lessons.title: Bargaining at the Vegetable Market
lessons.scene: Saturday morning at the Gandhi Bazaar vegetable market. A vendor sits behind heaps of tomatoes, beans and greens. You want tomatoes.

steps.speaker: Vendor
steps.kannada: Banni amma, fresh tomato ide! Enu beku?
steps.script: ಬನ್ನಿ ಅಮ್ಮ, ಫ್ರೆಶ್ ಟೊಮ್ಯಾಟೋ ಇದೆ! ಏನು ಬೇಕು?
steps.pronunciation: BAN-ni am-ma, fresh to-MYAA-to i-de! EH-nu BEH-ku
steps.english: Come madam, fresh tomatoes! What do you need?

steps.prompt: Ask how much the tomatoes are per kilo

choices.english: How much is a kilo of tomatoes?

choices.hint: They're right in front of you! To ask the price, use 'eshtu' (how much): 'kilo eshtu?'.
'Yaava' (which) doesn't fit. For price ask 'kilo eshtu?' (how much per kilo?).

wrongReaction.speaker: Vendor
wrongReaction.kannada: Illene amma, nim munde ne ide!
wrongReaction.english: Right here madam, it's in front of you!

steps.grammarTip: For prices, the pattern is simply '[item] [unit] eshtu?' — 'tomato kilo eshtu?'. Kannada drops 'is/are' and the word 'per'; context carries the meaning. Short and natural.

steps.prompt: Forty seems high — bargain politely to reduce the price

choices.hint: That asks for a higher price! To bargain down, say 'kammi maadi' (make it less) or name a lower number.

choices.english: Forty is just right, give it.
choices.hint: That accepts the full price — no bargaining! Push back with 'tumba jaasti, kammi maadi'.

correctReaction.speaker: Vendor
correctReaction.english: Thirty won't work madam, give thirty-five.

wrongReaction.speaker: Vendor
wrongReaction.english: Okay madam, how many kilos shall I put?

steps.grammarTip: Bargaining is expected and friendly. '[number] maadi' = 'make it [number]'. The vendor's 'aagalla' (won't work) is your cue to settle in the middle — here, thirty-five. Keep it light and smiling.

steps.speaker: You
steps.kannada: Sari, ondu kilo kodi. Phone pay maadli?
steps.script: ಸರಿ, ಒಂದು ಕಿಲೋ ಕೊಡಿ. ಫೋನ್ ಪೇ ಮಾಡ್ಲಿ?
steps.pronunciation: SA-ri, ON-du KI-lo KO-di. phone-pay MAAD-li
steps.english: Okay, give one kilo. Shall I pay by phone?

lessons.title: At the Clothes Shop
lessons.scene: You're at a clothes shop on Commercial Street looking for a cotton kurta. A salesperson approaches.

steps.speaker: Salesperson
steps.english: Come sir, what shall I show you?

steps.prompt: Ask him to show you a cotton kurta

choices.english: I don't want a cotton kurta.
choices.hint: That refuses it! To ask to see it, use 'torsi' (show me).

choices.english: Where is the cotton kurta going?
choices.hint: Odd phrasing. Simply say 'torsi' (show me) the cotton kurta.

correctReaction.speaker: Salesperson
correctReaction.kannada: Idu nodi saar. Yaava size beku?
correctReaction.script: ಇದು ನೋಡಿ ಸಾರ್. ಯಾವ ಸೈಜ್ ಬೇಕು?
correctReaction.pronunciation: i-du NO-di saar. YAA-va size BEH-ku
correctReaction.english: Look at this one, sir. Which size do you want?

wrongReaction.speaker: Salesperson
wrongReaction.english: What else shall I show you, sir?

steps.grammarTip: '-ali/-li' on a verb asks 'shall I...?': 'torsu' (show) → 'torsali?' (shall I show?). The shopkeeper uses it constantly. You reply with the polite command form ending in '-i': 'torsi' (show me).

steps.prompt: Tell him you want medium size, and ask the price

choices.english: I want medium. How much is this?
Not medium. How far is this?
choices.hint: 'Eshtu doora' asks distance, not price. For price just say 'idu eshtu?'; and you DO want medium, so 'beku'.

choices.english: I want large. Who is this?
choices.hint: Wrong size, and 'yaaru' (who) is for people. Ask 'idu eshtu?' for the price.

correctReaction.speaker: Salesperson
correctReaction.english: Five hundred rupees sir. Good quality.

wrongReaction.speaker: Salesperson
wrongReaction.english: Which size, do you want the price, sir?

steps.grammarTip: 'Idu' = this, 'adu' = that. 'Idu eshtu?' (how much is this?) is your everyday price question for any single item you're pointing at. Add 'bele' (price) for emphasis: 'idara bele eshtu?' = what's its price?
